parent.title: Killer serves for the amateur: a YouTube-anchored roadmap

siblings.title: Killer serves shortlist for amateur play
siblings.summary: Three high-leverage serves a club-level amateur should learn to win cheap points and set up the third-ball attack: short pendulum (backspin/no-spin pair), long fast no-spin to the elbow, and the short forehand tomahawk.

siblings.title: Best YouTube tutorials per serve
siblings.summary: One canonical YouTube video to learn each of the three killer serves (short pendulum + no-spin twin, long fast no-spin, forehand tomahawk), one for the reverse-pendulum upgrade, plus the four channels worth subscribing to.

siblings.title: Solo and partner practice progressions
siblings.summary: How a club-level amateur should build a killer serve: 15 min/day solo with paper targets and 10-in-a-row pressure, then partner drills that walk from fixed feeder return to fully random.

sources.title: tabletenniscoach.me.uk / Serve variation
sources.claim: Lodziak: identical pendulum action, contact while bat swings down → backspin; while rising → topspin.

sources.title: Larry Hodges / No-spin twin via contact point
sources.claim: Same swing, same wrist snap; tip → backspin, near handle → no-spin.

sources.title: Butterfly / Contact point on racket
sources.claim: Contact at base of paddle yields little spin; receiver mistakes no-spin for backspin and pops it up.

sources.title: Larry Hodges / Spin/no-spin pairs
sources.claim: Backspin/no-spin is the most popular twin: same motion, contact near handle for no-spin.

sources.title: ExpertTableTennis / Backspin on reverse pendulum
sources.claim: Top players hide spin pairs in subtle wrist differences; arm motion stays similar.

sources.title: PingSkills forum / Backspin on pendulum
sources.claim: Contact at the bottom of the down-then-up arc is hardest for the receiver to read.

sources.title: PingSkills forum / Pure heavy backspin
sources.claim: Drooping racket tip leaks sidespin into a 'pure' backspin and breaks the twin.

sources.title: PingSkills forum / Reading no-spin vs backspin
sources.claim: Disguise lives at contact: chop-shaped arm path with near-flat bat angle = no-spin.

sources.title: Human Kinetics / Mastering backspin serve
sources.claim: A deliberately abbreviated follow-through masks type and amount of spin.

sources.title: Larry Hodges / Semi-circular service motion
sources.claim: Semi-circular swing forces receiver to guess down/sideways/up at contact.

sources.title: Butterfly / Serving more deceptively
sources.claim: Mizutani uses different parts of the racket under near-identical swings; bounce/trajectory become the only reliable reads.

sources.title: PingSkills forum / Fake motions on serves
sources.claim: During-contact elbow misdirection beats purely-after fakes against good receivers.

sources.title: AllAboutTableTennis / Official rules
sources.claim: ITTF Laws 2.6.3-2.6.5: legal toss, ball not hidden, free arm clears.

sources.title: PingSkills forum / Double hit legal?
sources.claim: ITTF 2.10.1.7 penalises only DELIBERATE double strikes; air motion after one contact is fine.

sources.title: Uberpong / Bobrow trick serve
sources.claim: Bobrow brushes one direction, then dramatically flails arm the opposite way to mis-sell direction.

sources.title: Megaspin / 2002 service visibility rule
sources.claim: Serving player must not prevent receiver seeing ball or racket-side used.

sources.title: PingSunday / Contact point for max spin
sources.claim: EmRatThich: (1,2)/(2,2) handle zone = no-spin; near head = max spin.

sources.title: Larry Hodges / Reading service spin
sources.claim: Receivers take a 'mental flash photo' at contact; servers fake grazing then pat flat.

sources.title: Megaspin / Advanced serving
sources.claim: Forearm accelerates from backswing to contact; where you hit the paddle controls spin rate.

sources.title: Greg's TT pages / Spin on serves
sources.claim: Subtle changes to snap magnitude or direction → different spins from the same-looking action.

sources.title: MDPI / Wrist kinematics in serves
sources.claim: Higher wrist angular velocity → higher racket and ball speed; serve type differs in wrist flexion-extension and supination.

sources.title: Elevate Your Ping / Pendulum topspin
sources.claim: Snap forearm/wrist in pendulum, brushing fast across ball → builds deception across spin types.

sources.title: Megaspin / Serve like a pro
sources.claim: Flexible wrist + performance speed = magic-trick-style disguise.

sources.title: ExpertTableTennis / Keep serves short
sources.claim: Short serve: first bounce halfway between end-line and net or closer to net.

sources.title: ExpertTableTennis / Serve faster
sources.claim: Fast deep: first bounce near own end-line and corner.

sources.title: Larry Hodges / Fast deep serve
sources.claim: First bounce very close to own end-line, contact ball low from a foot behind table.

sources.title: Larry Hodges / Short serves
sources.claim: True short = bounces close to net on both sides.

sources.title: ExpertTableTennis / Half-long trap
sources.claim: Second bounce on or near receiver's end-line: too long to push short, too short to loop.

sources.title: Greg's TT pages / Double-bounce serve glossary
sources.claim: Half-long defined: second bounce within ~6 inches of receiver's end-line.

sources.title: PingSkills / Service disguise
sources.claim: Same preparation, similar swing across spin/placement options.

sources.title: PingSkills forum / Disguise short topspin
sources.claim: Strike just as bat starts upward; bounce close to body to mimic other-serve trajectory.

sources.title: AllAboutTableTennis / Advanced serve
sources.claim: High toss disguises short vs long; hidden long-fast can win the point outright.

sources.title: PingSunday / Chinese footwork
sources.claim: Recovery: serve, small jump, second small jump back to centre, before second bounce.

sources.title: Butterfly / Reverse serves
sources.claim: Hodges: develop reverse versions once regular pendulum/tomahawk/backhand serves are grooved.

sources.title: Butterfly / Reverse-adverse results
sources.claim: Cognitive switching cost: opponent must readjust to regular serves each time you alternate.

sources.title: PingSkills forum / Sense of reverse pendulum
sources.claim: Rosario: reads backhand serves easily but reverse pendulum is hard — exposure-driven.

sources.title: TableTennisDaily Academy / Reverse pendulum 5 steps
sources.claim: Same wrist-back/release plus matched waist/shoulder rotation; opposite direction sidespin.

sources.title: PingSkills forum / Reverse pendulum efficacy
sources.claim: Most disruptive at amateur/beginner level; advanced players counter the sidespin.

sources.title: PingSunday / Return sidespin
sources.claim: Sidespin curves return in opposite direction of spin — misread sends it off the table.

sources.title: ExpertTableTennis / Tomahawk serve
sources.claim: Tomahawk forces receiver wrist into ~45° contortion to return crosscourt.

sources.title: Lodziak / Tips on reading service spin
sources.claim: Bat moving down at contact = backspin; up = topspin.

sources.title: Butterfly / Reading service spin
sources.claim: Hodges heuristic: racket speed − ball speed = ball spin.

sources.title: RacketInsight / Foot stomping when serving
sources.claim: Foot stomp masks the audio tell distinguishing grazed (silent) from flat (knock) contact.

sources.title: arXiv 2409.11760 / Sound-based spin estimation
sources.claim: ML classifier reliably tells whether spin was applied from bounce sound alone.

sources.title: AllAboutTableTennis / Spin techniques
sources.claim: Logo visible on bouncing ball = little spin; blurred logo = heavy spin.

sources.title: ExpertTableTennis / Reading spin
sources.claim: Larcombe: even pros can't verbalize the visual difference; reading is exposure-based pattern matching.

sources.title: PingSkills forum / Reading spin/speed/placement
sources.claim: Rosario: two-channel input — contact and flight; exposure is the only fix.
